docs.text: Starts a FastAPI HTTP server that exposes durable project, pipeline, and validation records for inspection. The service is read-only and never mutates project state. Loopback-only by default; binding to a non-loopback address requires the explicit --allow-remote flag.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp serve PROJECT [--host HOST] [--port PORT] [--allow-remote]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT yes path Project root directory to serve

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Default Description --host HOST 127.0.0.1 Bind address; loopback by default --port PORT 8765 TCP port to listen on --allow-remote false Explicitly authorize binding to a non-loopback address

Remote access requires explicit authorization

Without --allow-remote, the server binds only to 127.0.0.1. Any non-loopback --host value requires this flag or the command fails.

docs.title: API endpoints
docs.text:
The service exposes read-only JSON endpoints for:

  • Project structure verification
  • Project state health check
  • Workflow records per function
  • Analysis database queries
  • Work queue status
  • Worker capability reports
  • Pipeline and stage status
  • Compilation reports
  • Evidence and claim records
  • Project memory entries

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Runs until interrupted (Ctrl+C), then returns {"stopped": true} on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error or project verification failure

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp serve ./my-project --port 8080

# With remote access authorized:
$ x86decomp serve ./my-project --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8765 --allow-remote

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• verify-project — Verify project structure and hashes
  • pipeline-status — Show pipeline status

docs.title: Pipeline Commands
docs.text: Durable analysis pipeline management with create, run, status, cancel, retry, and stale-recovery operations.

docs.title: pipeline-create
docs.text:
Create a default durable analysis pipeline manifest.

x86decomp pipeline-create PROJECT OUTPUT --without-ghidra
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root OUTPUT yes Path for the generated pipeline manifest file --without-ghidra no Exclude Ghidra export stages from the pipeline

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp pipeline-create ./myproject ./pipelines/default.json

# Without Ghidra stages
x86decomp pipeline-create ./myproject ./pipelines/no-ghidra.json --without-ghidra

docs.title: pipeline-run
docs.text:
Run or resume a durable project pipeline.

x86decomp pipeline-run PROJECT MANIFEST --continue-on-failure
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root MANIFEST yes Path to the pipeline manifest file --continue-on-failure no Continue running subsequent stages even if a stage fails (default: stop on failure)

A pipeline run persists its state. Re-running the same manifest resumes from incomplete stages.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp pipeline-run ./myproject ./pipelines/default.json

# Continue past failures
x86decomp pipeline-run ./myproject ./pipelines/default.json --continue-on-failure

docs.title: pipeline-status
docs.text:
Show durable pipeline and stage status.

x86decomp pipeline-status PROJECT PIPELINE_ID
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root PIPELINE_ID yes Pipeline run identifier

Returns the overall pipeline status and per-stage status, timing, and any error details.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp pipeline-status ./myproject run-20260711-001

docs.title: pipeline-cancel
docs.text:
Cancel a pipeline or one pipeline stage.

x86decomp pipeline-cancel PROJECT PIPELINE_ID --stage-id ID
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root PIPELINE_ID yes Pipeline run identifier --stage-id no Cancel a specific stage; if omitted, cancels the entire pipeline

Cancelled stages are not retried unless explicitly re-queued via pipeline-retry.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Cancel entire pipeline
x86decomp pipeline-cancel ./myproject run-20260711-001

# Cancel a specific stage
x86decomp pipeline-cancel ./myproject run-20260711-001 --stage-id ghidra-export

docs.title: pipeline-retry
docs.text:
Retry a failed pipeline stage.

x86decomp pipeline-retry PROJECT PIPELINE_ID STAGE_ID --cascade
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root PIPELINE_ID yes Pipeline run identifier STAGE_ID yes Stage identifier to retry --cascade no Also retry all downstream stages that depend on this stage

Tip

Use --cascade when a failed stage invalidates downstream results. Without it, only the specified stage is retried.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp pipeline-retry ./myproject run-20260711-001 ghidra-export

# Retry with all downstream stages
x86decomp pipeline-retry ./myproject run-20260711-001 ghidra-export --cascade

docs.title: pipeline-recover
docs.text:
Reset jobs with stale durable runner heartbeats.

x86decomp pipeline-recover PROJECT --pipeline-id ID --stale-seconds 600
Argument Required Description PROJECT yes Path to the project root --pipeline-id no Restrict recovery to a specific pipeline; if omitted, all pipelines are checked --stale-seconds no Heartbeat age threshold in seconds for a job to be considered stale (default: 600)

Stale jobs are reset to a pending state so they can be re-acquired by a runner.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Recover all stale jobs across all pipelines
x86decomp pipeline-recover ./myproject

# Recover a specific pipeline's stale jobs with a 5-minute threshold
x86decomp pipeline-recover ./myproject --pipeline-id run-20260711-001 --stale-seconds 300

docs.title: Worker Commands
docs.text: Local worker capability reporting and bounded compilation worker execution.

docs.title: worker-capabilities
docs.text:
Report local worker and tool capabilities.

x86decomp worker-capabilities

No arguments. Discovers and reports the local environment's worker capabilities: installed compilers, toolchains, Ghidra availability, Python version, platform details, and available validation backends.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp worker-capabilities

{
  "platform": "win32",
  "python_version": "3.11.4",
  "available_toolchains": ["msvc-2019-x86", "msvc-2019-x86_64"],
  "ghidra_available": true,
  "angr_available": false,
  "dynamorio_available": true
}

docs.title: compile-worker
docs.text:
Run a bounded local or containerized compilation worker.

x86decomp compile-worker PROFILE SOURCE OUTPUT \
  --isolation {local_bounded,container} \
  --container-image IMAGE \
  --cache PATH \
  --report PATH
Argument Required Description PROFILE yes Path to the compiler profile SOURCE yes Path to the source file to compile OUTPUT yes Path for the compiled output --isolation no Isolation mode: local_bounded (default) or container --container-image no Container image name (required when --isolation container) --cache no Cache directory for compiler artifacts --report no Path for the compilation report

Cross-reference

Related: compile — compile one source file under a declared compiler profile (non-worker variant).

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Local bounded compilation
x86decomp compile-worker ./profiles/msvc-2019-x86.json \
  ./src/00401000.c ./build/00401000.obj \
  --cache ./build/cache \
  --report ./reports/compile-401000.json

# Containerized compilation
x86decomp compile-worker ./profiles/msvc-2019-x86.json \
  ./src/00401000.c ./build/00401000.obj \
  --isolation container \
  --container-image msvc-2019:latest

docs.title: x86decomp init
docs.text: Initializes a native PE decompilation project from a Windows PE binary.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp init BINARY PROJECT [--reference-binary]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Parses the target PE (PE32 or PE32+), auto-detects the architecture, creates the full project directory layout, writes project.json, the program manifest (analysis/program.json), the host environment record (analysis/host.json), initializes the analysis database, work queue, project-state database, content store, and project memory ledger. Records the first project-memory event with the binary identity.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description BINARY Yes path Native Windows PE32 or PE32+ file to analyze. PROJECT Yes path Project root directory. Must be empty or non-existent.

docs.title: Options
docs.text:
Option Default Description --reference-binary off (binary is copied) When set, references the external binary path instead of copying it into PROJECT/original/.

Empty directory required

init rejects non-empty project directories. Use a fresh directory or an empty existing one.

docs.title: What it creates
docs.text:
Path Description project.json Schema v3 project identity, binary hash, paths to all subsystems. analysis/program.json Full PE metadata dump (parse_pe(...).to_dict()). analysis/host.json Python version, platform, and implementation at creation time. analysis/database/analysis.sqlite3 Empty SQLite analysis database. work/tasks.sqlite3 Empty SQLite work queue. state/project-state.sqlite3 Project-state database with schema-metadata row. artifacts/ Content store root directory. memory/events.jsonl Project memory ledger (first event written).

Plus the full 37-directory project layout (PROJECT_DIRS in src/x86decomp/project.py), including src/asm/, src/matched/, src/functional/, build/, evidence/, functions/, reports/, tests/, config/, orchestration/, target-pack/, templates/, and analysis/ghidra/.

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Success: emits the project dictionary as JSON on stdout, exit 0.
  • Failure: exits 2 with a JSON error on stderr (empty directory, invalid PE, etc.).

docs.title: Project ID convention
docs.text:
The project ID is constructed as: 

x86d-{sha256[:16]}-{uuid8}    # for x86 PE32
x64d-{sha256[:16]}-{uuid8}    # for x86-64 PE32+

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp init target.exe ./myproject

{
  "schema_version": 3,
  "project_id": "x86d-e3b0c44298fc1c14-1a2b3c4d",
  "created_at": "2025-07-11T12:00:00Z",
  "supported_scope": "native Windows PE32 x86",
  "architecture": "x86",
  "default_modes": ["matching", "functional"],
  "binary": {
    "name": "target.exe",
    "source_kind": "copied",
    "path": "original/target.exe",
    "sha256": "e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855",
    "size": 102400
  },
  "program_manifest": "analysis/program.json",
  "analysis_database": "analysis/database/analysis.sqlite3",
  "work_queue": "work/tasks.sqlite3",
  "memory_ledger": "memory/events.jsonl",
  "function_root": "functions",
  "evidence_root": "evidence",
  "project_state_database": "state/project-state.sqlite3",
  "content_store": "artifacts",
  "target_pack": "target-pack/target.toml",
  "orchestration_root": "orchestration",
  "toolkit_release": "0.7.11",
  "status": "initialized"
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• verify-project — validate the project after initialization
  • target-pack-infer — create a project from a target pack
  • project-check — check project state integrity

docs.title: x86decomp verify-project
docs.text: Validates a project's structure, binary hash, program manifest, and state database integrity.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp verify-project PROJECT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reads project.json, verifies the schema version, checks the declared binary path and SHA-256, reparses the binary against the recorded program manifest, validates the analysis database, work queue, project-state database, content store, and memory ledger.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root directory containing project.json.

docs.title: Checks performed
docs.text:
1. project.json exists and contains a valid object.
  2. Schema version is 1, 2, or 3.
  3. Binary file exists at the resolved path.
  4. Binary SHA-256 matches the recorded hash.
  5. Program manifest (analysis/program.json) exists.
  6. Program manifest fields (file_sha256, machine, image_base, entry_rva, number_of_sections, size_of_image, architecture) match the reparsed binary.
  7. For schema v2+: analysis_database and work_queue files exist.
  8. For schema v2+: default_modes is ["matching", "functional"].
  9. For schema v3: project_state_database file exists.
  10. For schema v3: content_store directory exists.
  11. Project memory ledger integrity check.

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Success: emits verification result as JSON on stdout, exit 0.
  • Failure: exits 2 with a JSON error on stderr.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp verify-project ./myproject

{
  "project_id": "x86d-e3b0c44298fc1c14-1a2b3c4d",
  "architecture": "x86",
  "valid": true,
  "failures": [],
  "binary_path": "/path/to/myproject/original/target.exe",
  "memory_event_count": 1
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• init — create the project
  • project-check — schema v3 integrity check via ProjectCheck
  • project-repair — repair reconstructible indexes

docs.title: x86decomp inspect-pe
docs.text: Parses PE32 or PE32+ metadata from a binary file.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp inspect-pe BINARY

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Parses the PE headers, sections, imports, exports, relocations, resources, and architecture. Returns the full PEImage.to_dict() representation. This is a read-only, non-mutating operation.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description BINARY Yes path PE32 or PE32+ file to parse.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: None.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp inspect-pe target.exe

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• pdb-inspect — inspect a companion PDB
  • coff-inspect — inspect individual COFF objects

docs.title: x86decomp pdb-inspect
docs.text: Inspects an MSF 7.0 PDB file and optionally matches it to a companion PE.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp pdb-inspect PDB [--pe PE_PATH]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Parses an MSF 7.0 (Multi-Stream Format) PDB file. When --pe is provided, matches DBI stream source information, section contributions, and public/global symbol records against the PE.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PDB Yes path MSF 7.0 PDB file to inspect.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --pe (none) Optional companion PE path for cross-referencing symbol and section information.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp pdb-inspect target.pdb --pe target.exe

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• inspect-pe — parse the PE itself
  • metadata-scan — recover MSVC RTTI/vtable metadata from PE and PDB
  • target-pack-infer — include PDB in a target pack

docs.title: x86decomp project-check
docs.text: Validates a project's on-disk state and collects integrity failures and warnings.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-check PROJECT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Reads project.json, checks schema version (must be 3), binary presence and hash, project-state database integrity and schema version, and content store integrity. This is a compatibility alias that delegates to x86decomp project check internally via _compatibility_alias.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root directory containing project.json.

docs.title: Return value
docs.text:
A ProjectCheck object serialized to JSON:

Field Description valid true when zero failures. failures List of failure strings (empty when healthy). warnings List of advisory warnings. project_schema_version Observed schema version from project.json. state_db_schema_version Observed schema version from state/project-state.sqlite3.

docs.title: Checks performed
docs.text:
1. project.json exists and is a valid object.
  2. Schema version is 3 (older versions produce a failure recommending migration).
  3. Binary file exists at the resolved path.
  4. Binary SHA-256 matches the recorded hash.
  5. Project-state database (state/project-state.sqlite3) exists.
  6. Project-state database passes PRAGMA integrity_check.
  7. State database schema version is read and recorded.
  8. Content store directory exists.
  9. Content store passes its internal integrity verification.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp project-check ./myproject

{
  "valid": true,
  "failures": [],
  "warnings": [],
  "project_schema_version": 3,
  "state_db_schema_version": 1
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• verify-project — broader verification covering schema v1–v3
  • project-migrate — migrate to schema v3
  • project-repair — repair reconstructible indexes

docs.title: x86decomp project-migrate
docs.text: Migrates project.json forward to schema version 3, with backup and audit trail.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-migrate PROJECT [--dry-run] [--backup BACKUP]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Supports migration from schema 1 or 2 to schema 3. Schema v3 adds the project-state database, content store, target-pack path, and orchestration root. Unless --dry-run is set, a pre-migration backup is created, the project file is rewritten, and the migration is recorded with a snapshot in the project-state database.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root containing project.json.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --dry-run off Compute and return the migration plan without modifying anything. Returns project_preview showing the projected post-migration state. --backup {PROJECT}-pre-migration-backup.tar.gz (sibling of project root) Explicit backup destination path.

docs.title: Migration paths
docs.text: From To Changes 1 2 Adds schema_version: 2, default_modes, analysis_database path, work_queue path. 2 3 Creates state/, artifacts/, orchestration/ dirs, target-pack/, report dirs, templates/. Initializes project-state database (schema v1) and content store. 3 3 No-op. Returns {changed: false, ...}.

docs.title: Return value (applied)
docs.text:
{
  "changed": true,
  "from_version": 2,
  "to_version": 3,
  "dry_run": false,
  "plan": ["2->3"],
  "backup": {"path": "/path/to/backup.tar.gz", "sha256": "...", "file_count": 42, "size": 512000},
  "snapshot": {"snapshot_id": "...", "project_json_sha256": "...", "state": {...}}
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Preview the plan
x86decomp project-migrate ./myproject --dry-run

# Apply with default backup location
x86decomp project-migrate ./myproject

# Apply with explicit backup
x86decomp project-migrate ./myproject --backup /safe/backups/myproject-pre.tar.gz

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• project-check — check current state before migration
  • project-backup — manual backup

docs.title: x86decomp project-backup
docs.text: Creates a deterministic gzip tar backup of the entire project tree.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-backup PROJECT OUTPUT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Recursively walks the project root, collects all regular files (sorted by path for determinism), and writes a gzip-compressed PAX-format tar archive with normalized metadata: uid=0, gid=0, uname="", gname="", mtime=0, mode=0o644, mtime=0 on the gzip stream.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root containing project.json. OUTPUT Yes path Output archive path. Must be outside the project tree.

docs.title: Safety constraints
docs.text:
• Rejects symlinks anywhere in the project tree.
  • Rejects output paths inside the project tree.
  • Writes to a temporary file first, then atomically renames to OUTPUT.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp project-backup ./myproject ./backups/myproject-2025-07-11.tar.gz

{
  "path": "/abs/path/to/backups/myproject-2025-07-11.tar.gz",
  "sha256": "abc123...",
  "file_count": 147,
  "size": 1048576
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• project-restore — restore from backup
  • project-migrate — creates a backup before migration

docs.title: x86decomp project-restore
docs.text: Safely restores a project backup into an empty destination.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-restore ARCHIVE DESTINATION

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Extracts a previously backed-up project from a gzip tar archive. Applies safety checks before extraction: rejects absolute paths, .. traversal, symlinks, hard links, device nodes, files over 2 GiB, total expansion over 8 GiB, more than 100,000 members, missing project.json, and non-empty destinations. Extracts through a staging directory, then moves contents to the destination atomically. Runs project-check on the restored project.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description ARCHIVE Yes path Gzip tar archive from project-backup. Must be a regular file. DESTINATION Yes path Empty directory to receive the restored project.

docs.title: Safety constraints
docs.text: Check Limit Archive member count ≤ 100,000 Single root directory Exactly one top-level dir No absolute paths or .. Rejected No symlinks, hard links, devices Rejected Per-member size ≤ 2 GiB Total expansion ≤ 8 GiB Destination must be empty Required Archive must contain project.json Required

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp project-restore ./backups/myproject-2025-07-11.tar.gz ./restored-project

{
  "restored": true,
  "destination": "/abs/path/to/restored-project",
  "archive_sha256": "abc123...",
  "check": {
    "valid": true,
    "failures": [],
    "warnings": [],
    "project_schema_version": 3,
    "state_db_schema_version": 1
  }
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• project-backup — create the backup
  • project-check — manual integrity check

docs.title: x86decomp project-repair
docs.text: Inspects or applies deterministic repairs to reconstructible project indexes.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-repair PROJECT [--apply]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Repairs only reconstructible indexes and missing empty infrastructure that can be derived safely. Never rewrites target bytes, evidence, claims, or candidate source. Repairs performed:

  1. Creates the project-state database if missing.
  2. Creates the content store directory if missing.
  3. Restores missing content-store artifact references from the state database.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root containing project.json.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --apply off (dry-run) When set, applies repairs. Without it, only reports what would be done.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Inspect what needs repair
x86decomp project-repair ./myproject

# Apply repairs
x86decomp project-repair ./myproject --apply

{
  "dry_run": true,
  "actions": [
    {"action": "create_content_store", "path": "/path/to/myproject/artifacts"}
  ],
  "changed": false
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• project-check — identify issues first
  • project-gc — garbage-collect content store

docs.title: x86decomp project-gc
docs.text: Garbage-collects unreferenced objects from the project content store.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-gc PROJECT [--apply]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Deletes only content-store objects that are not referenced by any active artifact reference. Defaults to dry-run mode for safety.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root containing project.json.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --apply off (dry-run) When set, actually deletes unreferenced objects. Without it, only reports reclaimable space.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# Preview reclaimable objects
x86decomp project-gc ./myproject

# Actually delete them
x86decomp project-gc ./myproject --apply

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• project-repair — repair before collecting
  • content-verify — verify content store integrity

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Target packs bridge the gap between a raw binary and a project. They record observed parser facts separately from user-supplied decisions, then drive template derivation and project generation.

docs.title: x86decomp target-pack-infer
docs.text: Infers a fact-preserving target pack from a primary PE image and optional supporting artifacts.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text:
x86decomp target-pack-infer PRIMARY_IMAGE OUTPUT_DIR
    [--name NAME]
    [--pdb PDB]
    [--map MAP]
    [--object OBJ...]
    [--library LIB...]
    [--rebuilt-image REBUILT]
    [--decisions DECISIONS]
    [--reference-artifacts]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Parses the primary PE and all supplied artifacts. Produces a self-contained target-pack directory with target.toml (identity, decisions, artifact inventory), observations.json (bounded parser output), image-profile.json (reference-hash-bound image layout), acceptance.json (matching/functional minima), and template-plan.json (adapter needs and unresolved facts). Inferences never invent compiler versions, linker flags, names, or source layout.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PRIMARY_IMAGE Yes path Native PE32 or PE32+ to analyze. Must be a regular file. OUTPUT_DIR Yes path Output directory for the target pack. Must be empty or non-existent.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --name (stem of PRIMARY_IMAGE) Human-readable target name written into target.toml. --pdb (none) Companion MSF 7.0 PDB. Source file extensions in the DBI stream are recorded as language evidence only (never promoted to decisions). --map (none) MSVC-compatible linker map (.map file). Parsed into observations.json. --object [] COFF object file. Repeatable. Each is parsed for architecture, section count, symbol count, and COMDAT sections. --library [] COFF archive/static or import library. Repeatable. Each is parsed for member count, symbol count, and object breakdown. --rebuilt-image (none) A previously rebuilt PE image. Compared to confirm same architecture, hash, and path recorded. --decisions (none) JSON file with explicit user decisions. Supported keys: preferred_mode, compiler_family, compiler_version, linker_family, source_language, allow_host_execution, target_description. Unknown keys raise an error. --reference-artifacts off (copies artifacts) When set, records external artifact paths without copying files into the pack. Less portable; use only when policy forbids copies.

docs.title: Decision fields
docs.text: Key Type Default Valid values preferred_mode string "both" matching, functional, both compiler_family string "unknown" Any string compiler_version string "unknown" Any string linker_family string "unknown" Any string source_language string "unknown" Any string allow_host_execution boolean false true / false target_description string "No target-specific description supplied." Any string

docs.title: Files written to OUTPUT_DIR
docs.text: File Description target.toml Schema v1 target identity, decisions, scope, and artifact inventory. observations.json Bounded parser output: PE metadata, PDB, linker map, COFF objects, libraries, rebuilt image, source language evidence. image-profile.json Evidence- and hash-bound image layout profile. acceptance.json Matching and functional acceptance minima. template-plan.json Required/optional external adapters and facts needing user confirmation. artifacts/ Copied artifact files (unless --reference-artifacts).

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Success: emits {target_pack, observations, output_directory} as JSON on stdout, exit 0.
  • Failure: exits 2 with a JSON error on stderr.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp target-pack-infer target.exe ./target-pack \
    --name "MyTarget" \
    --pdb target.pdb \
    --map target.map \
    --object obj1.obj --object obj2.obj \
    --library libc.lib \
    --decisions decisions.json

Example decisions.json: 

{
  "preferred_mode": "both",
  "compiler_family": "msvc",
  "compiler_version": "19.29",
  "source_language": "c++",
  "allow_host_execution": false,
  "target_description": "Windows game executable, circa 2003"
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• target-pack-verify — verify artifact integrity
  • template-derive — derive a template contract
  • project-from-target — generate a project

docs.title: x86decomp target-pack-verify
docs.text: Verifies a target pack's artifact integrity: file existence, hash matching, and size matching.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp target-pack-verify TARGET_PACK

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Loads target.toml, validates schema version, and for each artifact record verifies that the referenced file exists, is a regular file (not a symlink), its SHA-256 matches the recorded hash, and its file size matches. Also confirms the primary image hash matches primary_sha256.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET_PACK Yes path Path to a target.toml file, or a directory containing target.toml.

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Success: emits {valid, target_id, failures, artifact_count} as JSON on stdout, exit 0.
  • Failure: exits 2 with a JSON error on stderr (unsupported schema, invalid TOML).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
x86decomp target-pack-verify ./target-pack

{
  "valid": true,
  "target_id": "x86-e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8",
  "failures": [],
  "artifact_count": 3
}

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• target-pack-infer — create the pack
  • project-from-target — use the verified pack

docs.title: x86decomp project-from-target
docs.text: Initializes a decompilation project from a verified target pack.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp project-from-target TARGET_PACK PROJECT [--reference-binary]

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Verifies the target pack, resolves the primary image artifact, initializes a standard project with init, copies the entire target pack into PROJECT/target-pack/, updates project.json with the target pack path and template kind, copies Ghidra scripts if available, writes config/target-decisions.json, creates a default pipeline, and materializes the project template layout. Also writes a TARGET.md summary.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET_PACK Yes path Directory containing target.toml. PROJECT Yes path Project output directory. Must be empty or non-existent.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --reference-binary off (binary is copied) Passed through to the underlying init call.

docs.title: What it creates
docs.text:
Beyond the standard project layout, also produces:

Path Description target-pack/ Full copy of the target pack directory. config/target-decisions.json Schema v1 record of all decisions from the target pack. orchestration/pipelines/default.json Default durable pipeline manifest. ghidra_scripts/ Copied from the toolkit repository if available. TARGET.md Markdown summary with target ID, architecture, unknowns, and pipeline command.

The full template layout is also materialized (see template-materialize).

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Success: emits {project, target_pack_verification, project_root, pipeline, template} as JSON on stdout, exit 0.
  • Failure: exits 2 with a JSON error on stderr.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp project-from-target ./target-pack ./myproject

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• target-pack-infer — create the pack
  • template-materialize — materialize layout separately

docs.title: x86decomp template-derive
docs.text: Derives a grounded project-template contract from a target pack.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp template-derive TARGET_PACK

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Uses only verified pack contents and explicit decisions to calculate: enabled modes; exact assembly fallback need; whether object comparison, linker reconstruction, or whole-image comparison has enough supplied artifacts; whether compiler/linker identities are confirmed; whether dynamic host execution is authorized; evidence-backed source-language candidates; and unresolved blockers. Does not emit candidate function bodies or fake compiler profiles.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET_PACK Yes path Directory containing target.toml.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp template-derive ./target-pack

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• template-materialize — materialize the template into a project
  • target-pack-infer — create the pack that drives derivation

docs.title: x86decomp template-materialize
docs.text: Materializes the grounded working layout for an existing target project.

docs.title: Synopsis
docs.text: x86decomp template-materialize PROJECT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Creates the source, header, build, test, report, and configuration directories needed for the project's template kind (matching, functional, or hybrid). Writes placeholder guidance files (config/project-template.json, config/validation-policy.json, config/next-steps.json, scripts/project.py) that describe the template contract without inventing source code.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PROJECT Yes path Project root directory with project.json.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: x86decomp template-materialize ./myproject

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• template-derive — derive the contract first
  • project-from-target — does both verification and materialization

docs.title: C++ Recovery
docs.text: Recover C++ class relationships, vtable layouts, and MSVC metadata from a PE image.

docs.title: x86decomp cpp-recover
docs.text:
x86decomp cpp-recover PE \
    --metadata-report PATH \
    --object OBJ... \
    --map MAP \
    --report REPORT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Recover bounded C++ metadata and class relationships including RTTI hierarchies, vtable layouts, inheritance chains, and MSVC-specific metadata (unwind tables, TLS callbacks, initializer lists). Operates on a PE image with optional supporting evidence from COFF objects and linker maps.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PE yes path PE image to analyze --metadata-report no path Pre-computed MSVC metadata scan report (metadata-scan output) --object no path (repeatable) COFF object files for cross-referencing --map no path MSVC linker MAP file for symbol and layout evidence --report no path Write recovery report as JSON

docs.title: Recovery Pipeline
docs.text:
The tool combines multiple evidence sources:

  1. MSVC metadata — Runtime Type Information (RTTI), Complete Object Locators, vtable references extracted via metadata-scan
  2. COFF cross-references — Symbol tables and relocation entries from supplied objects
  3. MAP evidence — Public symbol names and address ranges

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
The report includes:

  • Recovered class hierarchy (base/derived relationships)
  • Vtable entries with resolved symbol names where available
  • Constructor/destructor associations
  • Inheritance type annotations (single, multiple, virtual)
  • Unresolved or ambiguous entries tagged with evidence provenance

Bounded recovery

Recovery is bounded to what the PE metadata exposes. RTTI may be stripped or partial in release builds. Unresolved relationships are reported as such with the available evidence.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp cpp-recover original/game.exe \
    --metadata-report metadata-scan.json \
    --object build/objects/game_main.obj \
    --object build/objects/game_entity.obj \
    --map original/game.map \
    --report cpp-recovery.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• metadata-scan — scan for MSVC RTTI, vtables, unwind, TLS metadata
  • coff-inspect — inspect COFF object symbols and relocations
  • map-inspect — parse MSVC linker map

docs.title: Execution Harness
docs.text: Generate an execution harness from binary functions for differential validation.

docs.title: x86decomp harness-generate
docs.text:
x86decomp harness-generate ABI_CONTRACT OUTPUT \
    --pointer-parameters PATH \
    --no-observe-pointers \
    --max-instructions 100000 \
    --timeout-ms 1000

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Generate an execution harness that can invoke a binary function under controlled conditions, observe its behavior, and compare it against a candidate implementation. The harness is guided by an ABI contract and optional pointer-parameter metadata.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Default Description ABI_CONTRACT yes path — ABI contract JSON defining calling convention, registers, stack layout OUTPUT yes path — Output harness file path --pointer-parameters no path — JSON file describing pointer parameters (regions, aliases) --no-observe-pointers no flag — Disable pointer-parameter observation in the harness --max-instructions no integer 100000 Maximum instructions to execute before forcing termination --timeout-ms no integer 1000 Wall-clock timeout in milliseconds

docs.title: Pointer Parameters
docs.text:
When --pointer-parameters is provided, the JSON must describe memory regions that the harness will initialize and observe:

  • Which parameters are pointers
  • Expected region sizes and alignments
  • Alias relationships between regions

When --no-observe-pointers is set, the harness treats all parameters as opaque values (no memory is allocated or compared).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp harness-generate abi_contracts/func_1234.json harness.bin \
    --pointer-parameters pointers.json \
    --max-instructions 50000 \
    --timeout-ms 2000

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• dynamic-validate — differentially execute with a harness
  • abi-check — validate behavior against an ABI contract
  • symbolic-validate — symbolic comparison (no harness needed)

docs.title: Image Matching
docs.text: Commands for whole-image layout profiling and PE image comparison.

docs.title: x86decomp image-profile
docs.text:
Derive a target-specific whole-image layout profile from a reference PE.

x86decomp image-profile REFERENCE OUTPUT

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description REFERENCE yes path Reference PE image OUTPUT yes path Write the derived profile as JSON

docs.title: Output
docs.text: A deterministic JSON profile describing the reference image layout: section headers, alignment, entry point, and structural properties used by image-match and relink comparisons.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp image-profile original/game.exe profile.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text: • image-match — compare images using this profile

docs.title: x86decomp image-match
docs.text:
Compare a candidate PE image against a reference under an explicit layout profile.

x86decomp image-match REFERENCE CANDIDATE --profile PROFILE --report REPORT

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description REFERENCE yes path Reference PE image CANDIDATE yes path Candidate (reconstructed) PE image --profile no path Layout profile from image-profile --report no path Write comparison report as JSON

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
A JSON comparison report with byte-level mismatch counts, section-level comparisons, and summary match status.

Relink integration

When used from x86decomp relink, the profile is read from the relink manifest's layout_profile field. The match report is embedded in the relink output for convergence tracking.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp image-match original/game.exe build/reconstructed.exe \
    --profile profile.json \
    --report match-report.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• image-profile — derive the layout profile
  • relink — relink and compare automatically
  • convergence-analyze — track convergence over time

docs.title: Linker Analysis
docs.text: Commands for parsing MSVC linker maps, correlating PE sections with COFF objects, and building linker reconstruction plans.

docs.title: x86decomp map-inspect
docs.text:
Parse an MSVC-compatible linker MAP file and emit structured metadata.

x86decomp map-inspect MAP

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description MAP yes path MSVC linker MAP file (produced with /MAP)

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
A deterministic JSON object containing:

Field Description timestamp MAP generation timestamp preferred_load_address Image base address contributions Per-object section contributions with RVA extents public_symbols Symbol names and their RVAs entry_point Program entry-point RVA object_order Ordered list of objects as seen by the linker

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp map-inspect build/game.map

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• layout-reconstruct — correlate MAP with PE and COFF objects
  • linker-plan — build a full reconstruction plan

docs.title: x86decomp layout-reconstruct
docs.text:
Correlate PE section headers, linker MAP contributions, and COFF object files.

x86decomp layout-reconstruct PE MAP [OBJECTS...] --report REPORT

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PE yes path Reference PE image MAP yes path MSVC linker MAP file OBJECTS no path (variadic) Zero or more COFF object files to correlate --report no path Write JSON reconstruction report

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
A JSON object with section-to-object mappings, contribution placements, unresolved entries, and object order. Each matched contribution carries an RVA extent and evidence tag.

Object matching

COFF objects are correlated to MAP entries by basename. Name rebuilt objects identically to the originals (e.g., main.obj, not main_matched.obj).

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp layout-reconstruct original/game.exe original/game.map \
    build/objects/crt0.obj build/objects/main.obj \
    --report layout-report.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• map-inspect — parse MAP alone
  • linker-plan — build a complete reconstruction plan

docs.title: x86decomp linker-plan
docs.text:
Build a grounded linker reconstruction plan from a PE image, MAP file, and COFF objects. Produces a relink manifest and readiness assessment.

x86decomp linker-plan PE MAP OBJECTS... \
    --library LIB... \
    --linker lld-link \
    --output-image build/reconstructed.exe \
    --report REPORT \
    --write-relink-manifest PATH

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Default Description PE yes path — Reference PE image to reconstruct MAP yes path — MSVC linker MAP file OBJECTS yes path (one or more) — COFF object files --library no path (repeatable) [] Static library archives for COMDAT resolution --linker no string lld-link Linker executable or command name --output-image no path build/reconstructed.exe Nominal output path for the relinked PE --report no path — Write the full JSON reconstruction plan --write-relink-manifest no path — Write a standalone relink manifest from the plan

docs.title: What the Plan Produces
docs.text:
The reconstruction plan records:

  • Reference image — PE path, SHA-256, architecture
  • Object order — evidenced link order from MAP, with unresolved entries
  • Placements — per-object, per-section RVA extents with evidence tags
  • COMDAT resolution — which object or archive provides each COMDAT symbol
  • Relink manifest — complete x86decomp relink arguments including linker, objects, /MACHINE, /BASE, /ALIGN, /SUBSYSTEM, /OUT
  • Readiness — ready_for_relink boolean and unresolved list

Readiness

A plan with ready_for_relink: false cannot be used with x86decomp relink until all unresolved items (missing objects, unknown subsystem, overlapping contributions) are addressed.

Complete link command not claimed

The plan records "complete_original_link_command_claimed": false. MAP files preserve object order and section placement but not every linker flag, response-file content, or environment variable.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp linker-plan original/game.exe original/game.map \
    build/objects/*.obj \
    --library build/libs/msvcrt.lib \
    --linker lld-link \
    --output-image build/reconstructed.exe \
    --report linker-plan.json \
    --write-relink-manifest relink.json

$ x86decomp relink relink.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• map-inspect — inspect MAP file contents
  • layout-reconstruct — layout correlation step
  • relink — execute the relink from the generated manifest
  • coff-inspect — inspect individual COFF objects

docs.title: Patch Image
docs.text: Patch a function body into a PE image at a given RVA, replacing the original bytes in-place.

docs.title: x86decomp patch-image
docs.text:
x86decomp patch-image ORIGINAL CANDIDATE OUTPUT \
    --rva RVA \
    --expected-original-sha256 HASH \
    --expected-function-sha256 HASH \
    --report REPORT

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Replace a function body in a PE image with a candidate byte sequence. The command rewrites the file at the target RVA, zeroes and recalculates the PE checksum, and writes a new output file — the original is never modified.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description ORIGINAL yes path Original PE image to read from CANDIDATE yes path File containing replacement function bytes OUTPUT yes path Patched output PE image --rva yes integer RVA of the function to patch --expected-original-sha256 no string Reject if original PE hash does not match --expected-function-sha256 no string Reject if original function bytes do not match --report no path Write patching report as JSON

docs.title: Hash Gates
docs.text:
Both --expected-original-sha256 and --expected-function-sha256 are optional safety gates. When provided, the command aborts with a ContractError if the respective hash does not match.

  • expected-original-sha256 — guards against using the wrong original PE
  • expected-function-sha256 — guards against patching a function that was already modified or incorrectly located

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
The in-place patching backend:

  • Cannot grow a function beyond its original byte footprint
  • Requires control-flow, unwind, relocation, and data references to already be valid for the patch location

Size constraint

The candidate byte sequence must not exceed the original function size. If the candidate is shorter, remaining original bytes are left untouched. Consider rebuilding the object and relinking for resized functions.

docs.title: Output Report
docs.text:
{
  "kind": "patch_image",
  "original": "...",
  "original_sha256": "...",
  "candidate": "...",
  "candidate_sha256": "...",
  "output": "...",
  "output_sha256": "...",
  "function_rva": 4096,
  "file_offset": 1024,
  "patch_size": 256,
  "original_function_sha256": "...",
  "pe_checksum": 0
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp patch-image original/game.exe candidate.bin build/patched.exe \
    --rva 0x1000 \
    --expected-original-sha256 abc123def456... \
    --expected-function-sha256 789abc012def... \
    --report patch-report.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• relink — full-image relink for multi-function changes
  • diff-function — compare PE function to COFF symbol
  • compile — produce the candidate bytes from source

docs.title: Relink and Hybrid Generate
docs.text: Commands for relinking from a reconstruction manifest and generating hybrid assembly projects.

docs.title: x86decomp relink
docs.text:
Relink an image from a declared reconstruction manifest.

x86decomp relink MANIFEST --report REPORT

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description MANIFEST yes path JSON relink manifest --report no path Write relink report as JSON

docs.title: Manifest Schema
docs.text:
The relink manifest (schema version 1) is typically produced by x86decomp linker-plan --write-relink-manifest. Required fields:

Field Type Description schema_version integer Must be 1 linker string Linker executable (e.g., lld-link) objects array of strings COFF object and library paths output string Output PE path arguments array of strings Linker command-line arguments

Optional fields:

Field Type Description reference_image string Path to reference PE for byte comparison map_file string Path to MAP file for layout reconstruction layout_objects array of strings Objects for layout correlation (defaults to objects) layout_profile string Path to image profile for whole-image match environment object Extra environment variables timeout_seconds integer Linker timeout (default: 300) inherit_environment boolean Inherit parent process environment (default: true)

docs.title: Linker Invocation
docs.text:
The manifest arguments array supports template tokens:

Token Substitution {output} Resolved output path {objects} Resolved object paths expanded inline {response_file} Path to a generated .rsp file listing all objects

All paths in the manifest are resolved relative to the manifest file parent directory.

docs.title: Post-Link Verification
docs.text:
On success, relink performs:

  1. Byte comparison — diff-bytes against reference_image
  2. Whole-image match — image-match if layout_profile is present
  3. Layout reconstruction — layout-reconstruct if map_file is present

docs.title: Output
docs.text: The report includes linker exit code, stdout/stderr, elapsed time, SHA-256 hashes, byte comparison results, image match results, and layout reconstruction results.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp relink relink.json --report relink-report.json

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• linker-plan — generate the relink manifest
  • diff-bytes — byte-level comparison
  • image-match — structured image comparison

docs.title: x86decomp hybrid-generate
docs.text:
Compatibility alias for the canonical x86decomp hybrid generate command. Generates a hybrid assembly project from a source project.

x86decomp hybrid-generate PROJECT OUTPUT \
    --architecture {x86,x86_64} \
    --asm-format {bytes,annotated,mnemonic} \
    --image-base 0 \
    --assembler-command-json JSON \
    --symbol-map MAP \
    --overwrite

Canonical route

This command delegates to the canonical hybrid capability group. It is functionally identical to x86decomp hybrid generate.

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Default Description PROJECT yes path — Source project root OUTPUT yes path — Output directory --architecture no x86, x86_64 x86 Target machine architecture --asm-format no bytes, annotated, mnemonic bytes Assembly output format --image-base no integer 0 Image base address --assembler-command-json no JSON string — Assembler invocation as JSON array --symbol-map no path — Symbol map for extern resolution --overwrite no flag — Overwrite existing output

docs.title: Assembly Formats
docs.text: Format Description bytes Raw machine-code bytes (conservative default) annotated Disassembly with address and evidence annotations mnemonic Human-readable mnemonic assembly

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp hybrid-generate my_project build/hybrid_out \
    --architecture x86 \
    --asm-format mnemonic \
    --image-base 0x400000 \
    --symbol-map symbols.json \
    --overwrite

Equivalent canonical form:

$ x86decomp hybrid generate my_project build/hybrid_out \
    --architecture x86 \
    --asm-format mnemonic \
    --image-base 0x400000 \
    --symbol-map symbols.json \
    --overwrite

docs.title: Related Commands
docs.text:
• x86decomp hybrid generate — canonical form
  • x86decomp asm — assembly capability group

docs.title: Security Audit Commands
docs.text: Deterministic, offline security auditing of source trees and dependency vulnerability scanning.

docs.title: x86decomp security-audit
docs.text: Audit a source tree for declared security checks.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Performs a deterministic offline audit of a source tree. Scans for hardcoded secrets (PEM private-key blocks, credential-like assignments), world-writable files, and other declared security patterns. The audit is deterministic and does not require network access. Vulnerability databases require an external scanner and are reported as unavailable rather than fabricated.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp security-audit ROOT [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description ROOT yes path Root directory of the source tree to audit

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --report REPORT none Write structured JSON audit report to this path

docs.title: Checks performed
docs.text: Check Description Secret patterns PEM private-key blocks (RSA, OpenSSH), credential-like assignments (aws_secret_access_key, client_secret) File permissions World-writable and setuid/setgid files Source inventory Full file listing with sizes and SHA-256 hashes

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp security-audit ./src --report reports/security-audit.json

docs.title: x86decomp dependency-audit
docs.text: Run an installed pip-audit adapter and preserve exact findings.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Invokes the pip-audit tool (or a declared alternative executable) against the current environment and captures its exact output. Findings are preserved in a structured report. The adapter reports unavailability rather than fabricating results when the tool is not installed.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp dependency-audit [--executable EXE] [--timeout SECONDS] [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: None required.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --executable EXE pip-audit Path or name of the pip-audit executable --timeout SECONDS 300 Maximum time in seconds before the audit is terminated --report REPORT none Write structured JSON audit report to this path

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error or tool execution failure
  • Reports tool unavailability as structured data, not as a fabricated pass

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp dependency-audit --timeout 600 --report reports/dependency-audit.json

$ x86decomp dependency-audit --executable pip-audit --report reports/dep-audit.json

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• security-audit — Source tree security audit
  • sbom-generate — Generate software bill of materials
  • release-manifest-verify — Verify release hash manifest

docs.title: Release Commands
docs.text: Software bill of materials generation and release hash manifest verification.

docs.title: x86decomp sbom-generate
docs.text: Generate a software bill of materials.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Inventories the installed toolkit distribution: Python package metadata, declared dependencies with versions, installed files, and any bundled tool executables. The output is a self-contained JSON document suitable for supply-chain audit and release attestation.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp sbom-generate OUTPUT

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description OUTPUT yes path Output path for the JSON SBOM document

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • OUTPUT — JSON software bill of materials

docs.title: SBOM contents
docs.text: Section Description metadata Release version and generation timestamp packages Installed Python packages with versions and dependencies files Installed file inventory with paths and hashes executables Bundled or referenced tool executables

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp sbom-generate release/sbom-0.7.11.json

docs.title: x86decomp release-manifest-verify
docs.text: Verify every entry in a release hash manifest.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Checks each file listed in a release hash manifest against the current filesystem root. Verifies that every declared file exists and matches its expected SHA-256 hash. Reports missing files, unexpected files, and hash mismatches individually.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp release-manifest-verify ROOT [--manifest PATH]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description ROOT yes path Root directory to verify against the manifest

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --manifest PATH none Path to the JSON release hash manifest

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• ROOT — filesystem tree to verify
  • --manifest PATH — JSON hash manifest declaring expected file paths and SHA-256 hashes

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error or any hash mismatch
  • Reports individual mismatches with path, expected hash, and observed hash

docs.title: Example
docs.text: $ x86decomp release-manifest-verify ./dist --manifest release/manifest-0.7.11.json

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• sbom-generate — Generate SBOM
  • security-audit — Source tree security audit
  • dependency-audit — Dependency vulnerability scan

docs.title: abi-check
docs.text: Validate observed behavior against an ABI contract.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Disassembles a binary code blob and statically checks whether the observed instruction patterns conform to a declared ABI contract. Verifies calling convention compliance: prologue/epilogue structure, register usage, stack frame management, parameter access patterns, and return value handling.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp abi-check CODE CONTRACT [--base BASE] [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description CODE yes path Binary code blob to analyze CONTRACT yes path JSON ABI contract specifying expected calling convention and behavior

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --base ADDR 0 Base address for disassembly (integer, supports 0x prefix) --report REPORT none Write structured JSON validation report to this path

docs.title: ABI contract format
docs.text:
The ABI contract is a JSON document declaring expected behavior:

{
    "architecture": "x86",
    "convention": "stdcall",
    "stack_argument_bytes": 4,
    "callee_stack_cleanup": 4,
    "variadic": false,
    "this_register": null,
    "register_arguments": [],
    "return_registers": ["eax"],
    "structure_return": false,
    "floating_point": "none"
}

docs.title: Accepted values
docs.text: Field Accepted values architecture x86, x86_64 convention cdecl, stdcall, thiscall, fastcall, vectorcall, unknown floating_point x87, sse, mixed, none, unknown stack_argument_bytes integer or null callee_stack_cleanup integer or null variadic boolean this_register register name string or null register_arguments array of register name strings return_registers array of register name strings structure_return boolean

docs.title: Validation checks
docs.text:
The validator examines the disassembled instruction stream for:

  • Prologue pattern (push ebp / mov ebp, esp, frame allocation)
  • Epilogue pattern (mov esp, ebp / pop ebp, ret with stack cleanup)
  • Parameter access relative to the expected frame layout
  • Caller vs callee stack cleanup matching the declared convention
  • Register usage consistent with the declared convention
  • Return register usage matching declared return type
  • Floating-point instruction presence matching the declared float mode

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• CODE — binary code blob
  • CONTRACT — JSON ABI contract

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • --report REPORT — structured JSON validation report

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout (pass or fail — both are valid results)
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp abi-check original/func.bin contracts/func-abi.json \
    --base 0x401000 \
    --report build/abi-check-report.json

With contract file:

{
    "architecture": "x86",
    "convention": "stdcall",
    "stack_argument_bytes": 8,
    "callee_stack_cleanup": 8,
    "variadic": false,
    "this_register": null,
    "register_arguments": [],
    "return_registers": ["eax"],
    "structure_return": false,
    "floating_point": "none"
}

ABI contracts and matching work

When you have a correct byte match from diff-function, the matching compiler profile gives you the calling convention. Use abi-check to validate that your reconstructed C source follows the same convention before attempting a byte match — mismatched conventions are a common cause of relocation mismatches.

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• diff-function — PE function to COFF symbol comparison
  • dynamic-validate — Differential execution via Unicorn
  • symbolic-validate — Symbolic equivalence via Z3

docs.title: diff-bytes
docs.text: Compare two files byte-for-byte with bounded mismatch reporting.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Performs an exact byte-for-byte comparison of two arbitrary binary files. Reports mismatched byte positions and values up to a configurable limit. Computes prefix and suffix matching spans and overall similarity ratio.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp diff-bytes TARGET CANDIDATE [--report REPORT] [--max-mismatches N]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET yes path Reference file (ground truth) CANDIDATE yes path File to compare against the target

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --report REPORT none Write structured JSON diff report to this path --max-mismatches N 64 Maximum number of mismatches to report (must be positive)

docs.title: Output structure
docs.text:
The comparison result includes:

Field Description match true if both files are identical, false otherwise length Lengths: {"target": N, "candidate": N, "common": N} prefix Number of matching bytes from the start suffix Number of matching bytes from the end (beyond prefix) similarity SequenceMatcher ratio (0.0 – 1.0) mismatches Array of {"offset": N, "target": B, "candidate": B} up to max_mismatches target_sha256 SHA-256 of target file candidate_sha256 SHA-256 of candidate file

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• TARGET — reference file
  • CANDIDATE — comparison file

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • --report REPORT — structured JSON comparison report

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp diff-bytes original/game.exe build/rebuilt.exe \
    --report build/diff-bytes-report.json \
    --max-mismatches 128

Report always reflects bounded analysis

When the number of mismatches exceeds --max-mismatches, the report captures the first N mismatches. The match field is still false. Increase the limit or use SHA-256 comparison (match derived from hash equality) as a fast pre-check.

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• diff-function — Compare a linked PE function to a COFF symbol
  • dynamic-validate — Differential execution comparison via Unicorn
  • symbolic-validate / angr-validate — Symbolic equivalence via Z3 or angr

docs.title: diff-function
docs.text: Compare a linked PE function body to a COFF object symbol.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Extracts a function body from a PE image at a given RVA and size, extracts the matching symbol from a COFF object (with relocation normalization), then compares them byte-for-byte. Also performs instruction-stream comparison. This is the primary matching-mode validation: "Does my compiled COFF symbol match the original PE function?"

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp diff-function PE RVA SIZE COFF SYMBOL [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description PE yes path PE32 or PE32+ image file RVA yes int Relative virtual address of the function in the PE (supports 0x prefix) SIZE yes int Size in bytes of the function body (supports 0x prefix) COFF yes path COFF object file containing the symbol SYMBOL yes string Symbol name to extract from the COFF object

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --report REPORT none Write structured JSON comparison report to this path

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
1. Parses the PE image and resolves the RVA to a file offset
  2. Extracts SIZE bytes from the PE as the target function body
  3. Parses the COFF object and extracts the named symbol payload
  4. Selects the matching COFF section with non-zero relocation entries
  5. Normalizes COFF relocations by zeroing relocation target bytes
  6. Performs byte comparison with max_mismatches=64
  7. Performs instruction-stream comparison using the toolkit disassembler
  8. Reports a classification: byte_matched, relocation_normalized_match, instruction_similar, or mismatch

docs.title: Output structure
docs.text: Field Description classification One of byte_matched, relocation_normalized_match, instruction_similar, mismatch byte_comparison Byte-level diff result (prefix, suffix, mismatches, similarity) instruction_comparison Instruction-stream comparison result normalized_similarity Similarity ratio after relocation normalization (0.0 – 1.0)

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• PE — PE image file
  • COFF — COFF object file

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • --report REPORT — structured JSON comparison report

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr
  • Exit 2 if the RVA is not mapped by any PE section

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp diff-function original/game.exe 0x401000 256 build/candidate.obj _ProcessInput@4 \
    --report build/diff-function-report.json

RVA and SIZE support hex

Both RVA and SIZE accept decimal or hex values. Prefix hex with 0x: 0x401000, 0x100. Values are parsed with int(value, 0).

Relocation normalization is not always sufficient

Some compiler/linker combinations produce differences beyond relocation fixups (e.g. merged jump tables, padding bytes, function alignment). A relocation_normalized_match classification confirms the same codegen with only relocation differences. Use instruction_similar as a strong indicator that the compiler and flags are correct.

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• diff-bytes — Byte-for-byte comparison of two arbitrary files
  • compiler-lab — Automated scoring across compiler profiles using this comparison
  • compile — Compile source using a compiler profile

docs.title: dynamic-validate
docs.text: Differentially execute and compare two binaries using the Unicorn emulator.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Runs both a target binary blob and a candidate binary blob through the Unicorn CPU emulator under identical initial register/memory state, then compares observable side effects (register values, memory writes, flags). Designed for bounded leaf-function comparison where external calls are represented by user-supplied deterministic stubs in the harness.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp dynamic-validate TARGET CANDIDATE HARNESS [--target-base BASE] [--candidate-base BASE] [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET yes path Reference binary function blob (ground truth bytes) CANDIDATE yes path Recompiled binary function blob to validate HARNESS yes path JSON harness specifying initial register state, memory layout, and stub hooks

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --target-base ADDR none Base address at which to load the target blob (integer, supports 0x prefix) --candidate-base ADDR none Base address at which to load the candidate blob (integer, supports 0x prefix) --report REPORT none Write structured JSON validation report to this path

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Requires the Unicorn CPU emulator engine. Install with:

$ pip install x86decomp-toolkit[dynamic]

Not a security boundary

This validator emulates bounded leaf routines. It intentionally does not model a complete Windows process, the filesystem, or the network. External calls are only modeled when the harness provides deterministic stubs.

docs.title: Harness format
docs.text:
The harness is a JSON document that describes:

  • Initial register values (eax, ecx, edx, ebx, esp, ebp, esi, edi, eflags, and x86_64 extended registers)
  • Memory pages with their contents and permissions
  • Hook functions for external calls with deterministic return behavior
  • Expected output: register values, memory regions to compare, flag masks

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• TARGET — reference binary blob
  • CANDIDATE — candidate binary blob
  • HARNESS — JSON execution harness

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • --report REPORT — structured JSON validation report

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout (match or mismatch — both are valid results)
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr
  • Exit 2 with ExternalToolError if Unicorn is not installed

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp dynamic-validate original/func.bin build/candidate.bin harnesses/func-harness.json \
    --target-base 0x401000 \
    --candidate-base 0x500000 \
    --report build/dynamic-report.json

Harness generation

Use x86decomp harness-generate to produce initial harness templates from an ABI contract and binary functions. Edit the generated harness to add stubs for any functions the target calls.

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• symbolic-validate / angr-validate — Symbolic equivalence via Z3 or angr
  • diff-bytes — Byte-for-byte comparison
  • diff-function — PE function to COFF symbol comparison
  • abi-check — Static ABI contract validation

docs.title: objdiff-run
docs.text: Run an objdiff-compatible comparison manifest.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Executes the external objdiff-cli tool (or a declared alternative) with the arguments, target, and candidate files specified in a version-1 manifest. objdiff's CLI/configuration evolves independently — this adapter executes an explicit argument array, captures provenance, and imports the declared output without hard-coding unstable flags.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: x86decomp objdiff-run MANIFEST [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description MANIFEST yes path JSON manifest (schema version 1) specifying target, candidate, and objdiff arguments

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --report REPORT none Write structured JSON report capturing objdiff provenance and output to this path

docs.title: Manifest format
docs.text:
The manifest is a JSON document with schema version 1:

{
    "schema_version": 1,
    "executable": "objdiff-cli",
    "target": "original/Game.exe",
    "candidate": "build/Game.exe",
    "arguments": [
        "diff",
        "--target", "{target}",
        "--candidate", "{candidate}",
        "--output", "{output}"
    ]
}
Field Required Description schema_version yes Must be 1 executable no objdiff executable name or path (default: objdiff-cli); resolved from PATH if not absolute target yes Path to the reference/ground-truth object or binary candidate yes Path to the rebuilt object or binary arguments yes Non-empty array of argument strings to pass to objdiff

docs.title: Argument tokens
docs.text:
The arguments array supports these substitution tokens:

Token Replaced with {target} Resolved absolute path to the target file {candidate} Resolved absolute path to the candidate file {output} Path to a temporary output file in the ephemeral work directory {workdir} Path to the ephemeral work directory

At minimum, {target} and {candidate} must appear in the arguments.

docs.title: Execution
docs.text:
1. Resolves the executable from PATH or an absolute path
  2. Verifies target and candidate files exist
  3. Creates a temporary work directory
  4. Expands argument tokens
  5. Runs objdiff as a subprocess with a timeout
  6. Captures exit code, stdout, stderr, and the output file if one was declared
  7. Records provenance (command line, timings, working directory) in the result

docs.title: Output structure
docs.text: Field Description exit_code objdiff process exit code elapsed Wall-clock and CPU time in seconds stdout Captured standard output stderr Captured standard error output Contents of the declared output file (if {output} was used and the file exists) manifest Copy of the manifest used resolved_executable Absolute path to the objdiff binary that was executed

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• MANIFEST — objdiff manifest JSON
  • Target and candidate files referenced in the manifest

docs.title: Files written
docs.text:
• --report REPORT — structured JSON run report
  • Declared output file via {output} token (may be in temporary directory)

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout (even if objdiff itself returns non-zero)
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr
  • Exit 2 with ExternalToolError if objdiff executable is unavailable
  • Exit 2 with ContractError if the manifest is invalid or target/candidate are missing

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp objdiff-run objdiff-manifest.json --report build/objdiff-report.json

With manifest:

{
    "schema_version": 1,
    "executable": "objdiff-cli",
    "target": "original/Game.exe",
    "candidate": "build/Game.exe",
    "arguments": [
        "diff",
        "--target", "{target}",
        "--candidate", "{candidate}",
        "--output", "{output}",
        "--format", "json"
    ]
}

objdiff is an external tool

objdiff-run is a real external-tool boundary, not a fake diff. The adapter does not hard-code objdiff flags — it passes through any argument array you declare. This means the manifest is version-bound to your installed objdiff CLI. Review the objdiff help (objdiff-cli --help) before writing a manifest.

Version coupling

objdiff's CLI changes between versions. If your manifest arguments are rejected by objdiff, check your objdiff version and update the argument array. The toolkit does not validate argument compatibility.

docs.title: Related commands
docs.text:
• diff-bytes — Direct byte-for-byte comparison (no external tool)
  • compiler-lab — Automated matrix comparison using compile+diff
  • diff-function — PE function to COFF symbol comparison

docs.title: x86decomp symbolic-validate
docs.text: Symbolically compare two bounded binary functions using Z3.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Lifts two x86 or x86-64 binary blobs into symbolic expressions, constructs an equivalence query with symbolic inputs fed through both implementations, and proves or disproves output equivalence using Z3. Bounded: limited to the instruction subset the executor models, explored paths, and selected output registers. A successful UNSAT result means no path diverges on the observed outputs — not that the functions are identical in all respects.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
x86decomp symbolic-validate TARGET CANDIDATE
    [--architecture {x86,x86_64}]
    [--input-register REG]...
    [--stack-argument-words N]
    [--output-register REG]...
    [--max-steps N]
    [--max-paths N]
    [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Required Type Description TARGET yes path Reference binary blob CANDIDATE yes path Candidate binary blob to validate

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Option Default Description --architecture {x86,x86_64} x86 Target instruction set architecture --input-register REG [] Register to mark as symbolic input (repeatable; e.g. eax, ecx) --stack-argument-words N 0 Number of 4-byte stack argument words to mark as symbolic (x86) or 8-byte (x86_64) --output-register REG none Register to compare after execution (repeatable); if omitted, all general-purpose registers are compared --max-steps N 1000 Maximum execution steps per path --max-paths N 64 Maximum execution paths to explore --report REPORT none Write structured JSON validation report to this path

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Requires Capstone and z3-solver. Install with:

$ pip install x86decomp-toolkit[symbolic]

docs.title: Accepted values
docs.text:
• --architecture: x86 or x86_64
  • --input-register: Standard x86/x86_64 register names (case-insensitive). Repeat for multiple registers.
  • --output-register: Same register set. If not specified, the result compares all modeled general-purpose registers.
  • --max-steps, --max-paths: Positive integers controlling the exploration bound
  • --stack-argument-words: Non-negative integer

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Field Description result equivalent if Z3 proved equivalence, divergent if a counterexample was found, bounded_by_steps or bounded_by_paths if exploration was truncated counterexample If divergent: concrete input values and output register values for both sides that differ steps_explored Number of steps taken before the result paths_explored Number of paths explored

docs.title: Files read
docs.text:
• TARGET — reference binary blob
  • CANDIDATE — candidate binary blob

docs.title: Files written
docs.text: • --report REPORT — structured JSON validation report

docs.title: Exit behavior
docs.text:
• Exit 0 on success with JSON result on stdout
  • Exit 2 on argument error with argparse diagnostics on stderr
  • Exit 2 on runtime error with JSON {"error": ..., "message": ...} on stderr
  • Exit 2 with ExternalToolError if Capstone or z3-solver is not installed

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp symbolic-validate original/math_func.bin build/math_func.bin \
    --architecture x86 \
    --input-register eax --input-register ecx \
    --stack-argument-words 1 \
    --output-register eax --output-register edx \
    --max-steps 500 --max-paths 32 \
    --report build/symbolic-report.json

docs.title: x86decomp angr-validate
docs.text: Compare binary functions with the optional angr backend.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Same interface and semantics as symbolic-validate, but uses angr and claripy as the symbolic execution engine instead of the built-in Z3 executor. angr handles a broader instruction set and may explore different paths, but results are scoped identically to the selected architecture, input/output registers, path/step limits, and code blobs.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
x86decomp angr-validate TARGET CANDIDATE
    [--architecture {x86,x86_64}]
    [--input-register REG]...
    [--stack-argument-words N]
    [--output-register REG]...
    [--max-steps N]
    [--max-paths N]
    [--report REPORT]

docs.title: Arguments and options
docs.text: Identical to symbolic-validate. All arguments, options, and defaults are the same.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Requires angr. Install with:

$ pip install x86decomp-toolkit[symbolic] angr

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
$ x86decomp angr-validate original/math_func.bin build/math_func.bin \
    --architecture x86 \
    --input-register eax --input-register ecx \
    --output-register eax \
    --max-steps 500 --max-paths 32 \
    --report build/angr-report.json

docs.title: x86decomp symbolic-memory-validate
docs.text: angr comparison with symbolic memory region bases and alias constraints.

docs.title: Purpose
